preamble: The shift from the voiceless velar stop [k] to the voiceless bilabial stop [p] typically occurs when the original sound is a labialized velar [kʷ] (produced with lip rounding). If a language loses the velar closure but retains the lip rounding, the sound 'slides' forward to become a pure bilabial stop. This is a classic example of labialization.

examples.note: The PIE labiovelar shifted to a bilabial stop in Greek before certain vowels (compare Latin sequor).
This systematic labialization of PIE *kʷ to *p is the defining feature of the 'P-Celtic' language branch.
The Latin 'qu' cluster labialized into a 'p' in Balkan Romance.

sources: Alkire, T., & Rosen, C. (2010). Romance Languages: A Historical Introduction. Cambridge University Press.
